label: tetlock-decompose
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: forecasting-tournament DECOMPOSITION only. Break the big contested
question into the smallest set of separately-answerable sub-questions (cruxes)
such that answering all of them substantially answers the big question. Each
sub-question must be something a researcher could investigate on its own. Do
not answer any of them.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The Sierra Vista Municipal Water District (invented) serves ~140,000 people in a chronically drought-stressed service area. After a rate study and a majority-protest hearing that drew protests from ~9% of parcels, the board adopted a "Water Resilience Surcharge": $0.85 per hundred cubic feet on residential usage above Tier 2 (the surcharge falls on roughly the top 22% of residential users), projected to raise $4.1M/year into a restricted fund spent as follows: 55% on turf-replacement and high-efficiency-appliance rebates that prioritize low-income customers; 30% on debt service for a recycled-water plant; 15% on "water ambassador" conservation-education staff.

The rate study concludes that high-tier consumption imposes marginal supply costs roughly 1.6× base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases), but it derives the $0.85 figure in part from what it calls "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than strictly from cost allocation, and the district concedes the rebate program mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge. A ratepayer association has sued, alleging the surcharge exceeds the proportional cost of service attributable to the payers' parcels in violation of article XIII D, section 6(b) of the California Constitution, citing Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano. The district defends the structure under its conservation mandate (Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375) and argues that, in the alternative, the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all.

## The question

Does the Water Resilience Surcharge violate article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements? How does Capistrano apply to a tier surcharge that is partly cost-justified (1.6×) and partly incentive-priced, with proceeds partially redistributed as rebates to non-payers? Can article X, section 2's conservation mandate be reconciled with 218, or does 218 win? Is there a colorable argument the surcharge is a tax requiring voter approval (Prop 26), or a non-property-related charge? What is the litigation exposure (refunds, fees) and how could the structure be repaired?

Decompose this into sub-questions/cruxes.
text: {"subquestions":[{"id":"Q1","text":"Is the Water Resilience Surcharge a 'property-related fee' governed by Article XIII D, section 6(b) at all, or could it instead be classified as a tax requiring Proposition 26/218 voter approval, or fall under some other exemption (e.g., tied to Water Code §375 conservation-rate authority)?","why_it_matters":"This is the threshold gate for the whole dispute. If it's not a property-related fee (the district's alternative argument), §6(b)'s cost-of-service/proportionality test never applies and the analysis shifts entirely to Prop 26's 'reasonable relationship' fee-exceptions test or to whether voter approval was required and skipped. Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Vial treats water service charges as property-related fees, but a surcharge layered on top with conservation/redistributive purposes could be argued to function differently (e.g., closer to a regulatory/incentive charge). Getting this wrong misdirects the entire remaining analysis.","what_would_have_to_be_true":"Would need to research: (1) whether case law (Bighorn, Richmond v. Shasta Community Services Dist., Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency) treats volumetric water-usage surcharges as 'fees for a property related service' under Art. XIII D §6(b)(4); (2) whether any exception in Cal. Const. art. XIII C §1(e) (Prop 26) or Water Code §375 removes it from standard fee analysis; (3) whether the district's 'not a property-related fee at all' argument has succeeded in any comparable published case."},{"id":"Q2","text":"Under Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano and its progeny, does a tiered rate component partly justified by documented marginal cost (1.6×) and partly by independent 'conservation-incentive pricing' satisfy §6(b)(3)'s requirement that a fee 'not exceed the proportional cost of service attributable to the parcel,' or does the incentive-pricing increment itself constitute a per se violation?","why_it_matters":"This is the doctrinal core of the case. Capistrano held tiered rates must be based on the actual cost of providing service at that tier, not on conservation-goal pricing alone, but left open how much a district can rely on demand-management/conservation rationale if some tiering is cost-based. Whether courts require a dollar-for-dollar cost showing for every increment of the $0.85 figure — or permit some conservation-based premium as long as a substantial cost nexus exists — determines whether the surcharge (or what portion of it) is defensible.","what_would_have_to_be_true":"Would need to verify: (1) Capistrano's precise holding and reasoning distinguishing cost-of-service from conservation pricing; (2) whether Coziahr v. Otay Water District (2024) or similar post-Capistrano appellate decisions specifically addressed conservation-tiered surcharges and articulated a permissible-blend standard; (3) whether any published opinion allows a district to justify a rate premium above marginal cost via Art. X §2 conservation goals without violating §6(b)(3); (4) how courts treat a rate study that expressly labels part of its methodology as non-cost-based."},{"id":"Q3","text":"Does directing a majority (55%) of surcharge revenue to rebates and programs that predominantly benefit customers other than the surcharge-payers violate §6(b)(2)'s requirement that fee revenues 'shall not exceed the funds required to provide the property related service,' or §6(b)(3)'s proportionality requirement — i.e., is cross-subsidization from high-tier payers to non-payer rebate recipients categorically impermissible under Prop 218?","why_it_matters":"Even if the $0.85 rate could be justified purely by cost-of-service, redirecting most of the money to benefit different customers (rather than the class actually causing the marginal cost) is a distinct and potentially independently fatal defect — it suggests the charge functions as a redistributive/regulatory tax rather than a fee tied to service cost causation. This is likely the ratepayer association's strongest textual argument and needs separate analysis from the rate-setting methodology question in Q2.","what_would_have_to_be_true":"Would need to determine: (1) whether case law requires revenue from a property-related fee to be spent on services provided to the payor class specifically, or whether district-wide conservation benefit (shared aquifer/supply reliability) satisfies the nexus even if direct rebates flow elsewhere; (2) how courts have treated similarly structured 'polluter pays into a mitigation fund for others' fee designs; (3) whether the debt-service (30%) and ambassador-staff (15%) allocations are more defensible than the 55% rebate allocation because they arguably serve system-wide supply/conservation service to all ratepayers including payors."},{"id":"Q4","text":"Can Article X, section 2's constitutional conservation mandate (and Water Code §375's tiered/conservation-rate authorization) be harmonized with Article XIII D §6(b), or does §6(b)'s specific proportionality requirement control over the more general conservation mandate when the two are argued to conflict?","why_it_matters":"The district's primary defense rests on reconciling two constitutional provisions. If courts treat Art. X §2 as merely informing what counts as a legitimate 'service' cost (e.g., drought-reserve and peaking capacity costs bundled into the 1.6× multiplier) rather than as an independent basis for a conservation-pricing premium, the defense collapses into Q2. If some deference to the conservation mandate survives Prop 218, the surcharge has a stronger footing.","what_would_have_to_be_true":"Would need to verify: (1) how Capistrano itself addressed the Art. X §2 argument (it was raised and, per general understanding, rejected as not overriding §6(b) — verify exact treatment); (2) whether any later case (e.g., Santa Cruz, Palmdale Water Dist., or California Farm Bureau Fed'n v. State Water Resources Control Bd.) has found a path for conservation mandates to modify Prop 218 cost limits; (3) canons of constitutional interpretation for reconciling a general mandate (Art. X §2, added 1928) against a more specific, later-enacted limitation (Art. XIII D, added 1996) — specific-controls-general and later-in-time principles."},{"id":"Q5","text":"If a court finds some or all of the surcharge violates §6(b), what remedies and exposure follow — full invalidation vs. severance of the 'excess'/incentive-pricing increment, refund liability (measure and look-back period), and attorney-fee shifting — and how does §6(b)(5)'s burden-shifting to the agency affect litigation posture and settlement value?","why_it_matters":"This determines practical stakes for the district (potential refund of a material share of $4.1M/year plus fees) and for the ratepayer association's likely negotiating leverage, and it clarifies whether the fix could be prospective-only, which matters for advising the district now.","what_would_have_to_be_true":"Would need to research: (1) refund mechanics under Gov. Code §§ 53750 et seq. (Prop 218 Omnibus Implementation Act) and any applicable claims-presentation requirements/limitations periods (Gov. Code §§900 et seq., and the ~120-day or statute-specific periods for rate challenges, e.g., Gov. Code §66022/CCP §860 validation-action timing where applicable); (2) whether courts sever only the incentive-priced increment above the 1.6× cost figure and order a partial refund, as opposed to voiding the whole surcharge (look at Capistrano's remedy on remand and Griffith); (3) likelihood of CCP §1021.5 private-attorney-general fee award given public-interest litigation posture and the express burden-shifting in §6(b)(5)."},{"id":"Q6","text":"What rate-design and revenue-allocation changes would bring the surcharge into Prop 218/26 compliance, and would implementing them require a new Prop 218 notice-and-hearing process or Prop 26/218 voter approval?","why_it_matters":"This converts the legal analysis into an actionable remediation path for the district, which is the ultimate practical payoff of the litigation forecast — needed to advise on both defense strategy (can the district moot the claims by amending the rate before judgment?) and prospective compliance.","what_would_have_to_be_true":"Would need to determine: (1) whether the district could re-derive the $0.85 figure purely from documented marginal cost (capping it near the 1.6× multiplier) and thereby cure the §6(b)(3) problem; (2) whether restricting surcharge-fund use to costs of service for the paying tier (or moving the low-income rebate program to general fund / grant / voter-approved tax revenue) cures the §6(b)(2) cross-subsidy problem; (3) whether any rate modification of this kind triggers a fresh Article XIII D §6(a) notice-and-majority-protest proceeding, and whether the existing 9% protest record has any preclusive or evidentiary relevance to a new proceeding."}]}

subquestions.text: Is the Water Resilience Surcharge a 'property-related fee' governed by Article XIII D, section 6(b) at all, or could it instead be classified as a tax requiring Proposition 26/218 voter approval, or fall under some other exemption (e.g., tied to Water Code §375 conservation-rate authority)?
subquestions.why_it_matters: This is the threshold gate for the whole dispute. If it's not a property-related fee (the district's alternative argument), §6(b)'s cost-of-service/proportionality test never applies and the analysis shifts entirely to Prop 26's 'reasonable relationship' fee-exceptions test or to whether voter approval was required and skipped. Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Vial treats water service charges as property-related fees, but a surcharge layered on top with conservation/redistributive purposes could be argued to function differently (e.g., closer to a regulatory/incentive charge). Getting this wrong misdirects the entire remaining analysis.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Would need to research: (1) whether case law (Bighorn, Richmond v. Shasta Community Services Dist., Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency) treats volumetric water-usage surcharges as 'fees for a property related service' under Art. XIII D §6(b)(4); (2) whether any exception in Cal. Const. art. XIII C §1(e) (Prop 26) or Water Code §375 removes it from standard fee analysis; (3) whether the district's 'not a property-related fee at all' argument has succeeded in any comparable published case.

subquestions.text: Under Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano and its progeny, does a tiered rate component partly justified by documented marginal cost (1.6×) and partly by independent 'conservation-incentive pricing' satisfy §6(b)(3)'s requirement that a fee 'not exceed the proportional cost of service attributable to the parcel,' or does the incentive-pricing increment itself constitute a per se violation?
subquestions.why_it_matters: This is the doctrinal core of the case. Capistrano held tiered rates must be based on the actual cost of providing service at that tier, not on conservation-goal pricing alone, but left open how much a district can rely on demand-management/conservation rationale if some tiering is cost-based. Whether courts require a dollar-for-dollar cost showing for every increment of the $0.85 figure — or permit some conservation-based premium as long as a substantial cost nexus exists — determines whether the surcharge (or what portion of it) is defensible.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Would need to verify: (1) Capistrano's precise holding and reasoning distinguishing cost-of-service from conservation pricing; (2) whether Coziahr v. Otay Water District (2024) or similar post-Capistrano appellate decisions specifically addressed conservation-tiered surcharges and articulated a permissible-blend standard; (3) whether any published opinion allows a district to justify a rate premium above marginal cost via Art. X §2 conservation goals without violating §6(b)(3); (4) how courts treat a rate study that expressly labels part of its methodology as non-cost-based.

subquestions.text: Does directing a majority (55%) of surcharge revenue to rebates and programs that predominantly benefit customers other than the surcharge-payers violate §6(b)(2)'s requirement that fee revenues 'shall not exceed the funds required to provide the property related service,' or §6(b)(3)'s proportionality requirement — i.e., is cross-subsidization from high-tier payers to non-payer rebate recipients categorically impermissible under Prop 218?
subquestions.why_it_matters: Even if the $0.85 rate could be justified purely by cost-of-service, redirecting most of the money to benefit different customers (rather than the class actually causing the marginal cost) is a distinct and potentially independently fatal defect — it suggests the charge functions as a redistributive/regulatory tax rather than a fee tied to service cost causation. This is likely the ratepayer association's strongest textual argument and needs separate analysis from the rate-setting methodology question in Q2.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Would need to determine: (1) whether case law requires revenue from a property-related fee to be spent on services provided to the payor class specifically, or whether district-wide conservation benefit (shared aquifer/supply reliability) satisfies the nexus even if direct rebates flow elsewhere; (2) how courts have treated similarly structured 'polluter pays into a mitigation fund for others' fee designs; (3) whether the debt-service (30%) and ambassador-staff (15%) allocations are more defensible than the 55% rebate allocation because they arguably serve system-wide supply/conservation service to all ratepayers including payors.

subquestions.text: Can Article X, section 2's constitutional conservation mandate (and Water Code §375's tiered/conservation-rate authorization) be harmonized with Article XIII D §6(b), or does §6(b)'s specific proportionality requirement control over the more general conservation mandate when the two are argued to conflict?
subquestions.why_it_matters: The district's primary defense rests on reconciling two constitutional provisions. If courts treat Art. X §2 as merely informing what counts as a legitimate 'service' cost (e.g., drought-reserve and peaking capacity costs bundled into the 1.6× multiplier) rather than as an independent basis for a conservation-pricing premium, the defense collapses into Q2. If some deference to the conservation mandate survives Prop 218, the surcharge has a stronger footing.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Would need to verify: (1) how Capistrano itself addressed the Art. X §2 argument (it was raised and, per general understanding, rejected as not overriding §6(b) — verify exact treatment); (2) whether any later case (e.g., Santa Cruz, Palmdale Water Dist., or California Farm Bureau Fed'n v. State Water Resources Control Bd.) has found a path for conservation mandates to modify Prop 218 cost limits; (3) canons of constitutional interpretation for reconciling a general mandate (Art. X §2, added 1928) against a more specific, later-enacted limitation (Art. XIII D, added 1996) — specific-controls-general and later-in-time principles.

subquestions.text: If a court finds some or all of the surcharge violates §6(b), what remedies and exposure follow — full invalidation vs. severance of the 'excess'/incentive-pricing increment, refund liability (measure and look-back period), and attorney-fee shifting — and how does §6(b)(5)'s burden-shifting to the agency affect litigation posture and settlement value?
subquestions.why_it_matters: This determines practical stakes for the district (potential refund of a material share of $4.1M/year plus fees) and for the ratepayer association's likely negotiating leverage, and it clarifies whether the fix could be prospective-only, which matters for advising the district now.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Would need to research: (1) refund mechanics under Gov. Code §§ 53750 et seq. (Prop 218 Omnibus Implementation Act) and any applicable claims-presentation requirements/limitations periods (Gov. Code §§900 et seq., and the ~120-day or statute-specific periods for rate challenges, e.g., Gov. Code §66022/CCP §860 validation-action timing where applicable); (2) whether courts sever only the incentive-priced increment above the 1.6× cost figure and order a partial refund, as opposed to voiding the whole surcharge (look at Capistrano's remedy on remand and Griffith); (3) likelihood of CCP §1021.5 private-attorney-general fee award given public-interest litigation posture and the express burden-shifting in §6(b)(5).

subquestions.text: What rate-design and revenue-allocation changes would bring the surcharge into Prop 218/26 compliance, and would implementing them require a new Prop 218 notice-and-hearing process or Prop 26/218 voter approval?
subquestions.why_it_matters: This converts the legal analysis into an actionable remediation path for the district, which is the ultimate practical payoff of the litigation forecast — needed to advise on both defense strategy (can the district moot the claims by amending the rate before judgment?) and prospective compliance.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Would need to determine: (1) whether the district could re-derive the $0.85 figure purely from documented marginal cost (capping it near the 1.6× multiplier) and thereby cure the §6(b)(3) problem; (2) whether restricting surcharge-fund use to costs of service for the paying tier (or moving the low-income rebate program to general fund / grant / voter-approved tax revenue) cures the §6(b)(2) cross-subsidy problem; (3) whether any rate modification of this kind triggers a fresh Article XIII D §6(a) notice-and-majority-protest proceeding, and whether the existing 9% protest record has any preclusive or evidentiary relevance to a new proceeding.
